---
title: "Writing in Quarto"
subtitle: "Markdown, Citations, and Cross-References"
---

## Overview

::: incremental
- Markdown basics: text formatting and structure
- Headers and document organisation
- Citations and APA formatting
- Cross-referencing figures, tables, and sections
- Numbered example sentences
- Inline R for dynamic reporting
:::

---

# Markdown basics {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
*Text formatting and structure*
:::

## Text formatting

| Markdown | Output |
|---|---|
| `**bold**` | **bold** |
| `*italic*` | *italic* |
| `***bold italic***` | ***bold italic*** |
| `` `code` `` | `code` |
| `~~strikethrough~~` | ~~strikethrough~~ |
| `H~2~O` | H~2~O |
| `R^2^` | R^2^ |

## Headers
````markdown
# Chapter title (H1)
## Section (H2)
### Subsection (H3)
#### Subsubsection (H4)
````

. . .

::: callout-tip
In a Quarto book, H1 (`#`) is reserved for the chapter title in the YAML. Use H2 (`##`) for your first-level sections within a chapter.
:::

## Lists
````markdown
- unordered item
- another item
  - nested item

1. ordered item
2. another item
   1. nested item
````

. . .
````markdown
::: incremental
- appears
- one
- at a time
:::
````

## Block elements
````markdown
> This is a blockquote.
> Useful for quoting stimuli or definitions.
````

. . .
````markdown
::: callout-tip
A tip callout.
:::
````
::: callout-tip
A tip callout.
:::

````markdown
::: callout-note
A note callout — good for asides and tips.
:::
````
::: callout-note
A note callout — good for asides and tips.
:::

````markdown
::: callout-warning
A warning callout.
:::
````

::: callout-warning
A warning callout.
:::

````markdown
::: callout-important
An important callout.
:::
````

::: callout-important
An important callout.
:::

---

# Citations {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
*BibTeX, Zotero, and APA formatting*
:::

## Setting up a bibliography

In your YAML:
````yaml
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
````

. . .

`references.bib` — BibTeX file with your references  
`apa.csl` — Citation Style Language file for APA formatting

. . .

::: callout-tip
Download `apa.csl` from the [Zotero CSL repository](https://www.zotero.org/styles) or run:
````r
download.file(
  "https://www.zotero.org/styles/apa",
  here::here("apa.csl")
)
````
:::

## Citation syntax

| Syntax | Output |
|---|---|
| `@Bates2015` | Bates et al. (2015) |
| `[@Bates2015]` | (Bates et al., 2015) |
| `[-@Bates2015]` | (2015) |
| `[@Bates2015, p. 12]` | (Bates et al., 2015, p. 12) |
| `[@Bates2015; @Gelman2020]` | (Bates et al., 2015; Gelman, 2020) |

. . .
````markdown
Mixed-effects models were fitted following @Bates2015.
All analyses were conducted in R [@R2024].
````

## Getting BibTeX entries

::: incremental
- **Zotero** — export library or single item as BibTeX (recommended)
- **Google Scholar** — Cite → BibTeX → copy into `references.bib`
- **DOI** — use `doi2bib.org` to generate BibTeX from a DOI
- **R packages** — `citation("lme4")` gives a citable reference
:::

. . .
````r
citation("lme4")
toBibtex(citation("lme4"))
````

## APA formatting tips

::: incremental
- Use `csl: apa.csl` — Quarto handles the rest automatically
- Author-date in text: `@Bates2015` → Bates et al. (2015)
- References section: add `references.qmd` with `::: {#refs} :::`
- Running head, double spacing, line spacing: set in YAML or reference `.docx`
:::

. . .

::: callout-note
For APA 7th edition PDF output, use `documentclass: apa7` in your YAML and the `apa7` LaTeX class.
:::

---

# Cross-references {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
*Figures, tables, sections, and equations*
:::

## Cross-reference prefixes

| Type | Label prefix | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Figure | `fig-` | `@fig-mass` |
| Table | `tbl-` | `@tbl-demo` |
| Section | `sec-` | `@sec-results` |
| Equation | `eq-` | `@eq-model` |
| Example | `exm-` | `@exm-stimulus` |

## Figures
````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: fig-mass
#| fig-cap: "Body mass by condition."
ggplot(...)
```
````

. . .

In prose:
````markdown
As shown in @fig-mass, body mass differed by condition.
````

→ "As shown in Figure 1, body mass differed by condition."

## Tables
````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: tbl-desc
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics by condition."
tbl3 |> gt()
```
````

. . .
````markdown
Descriptive statistics are presented in @tbl-desc.
````

→ "Descriptive statistics are presented in Table 1."

## Sections
````markdown
## Results {#sec-results}

## Discussion {#sec-discussion}
````

. . .
````markdown
We return to this point in @sec-discussion.
````

→ "We return to this point in Section 4."

## Equations
````markdown
$$
y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{Tense}_i + \beta_2 \text{Lifetime}_i + \varepsilon_i
$$ {#eq-model}
````

. . .
````markdown
The model is specified in @eq-model.
````

→ "The model is specified in Equation 1."


# Numbered examples {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
*Linguistic example sentences*
:::

## Numbered example sentences

Quarto doesn't have built-in linguistic numbering, but you can use ordered lists with a custom class:
````markdown
(@) The cat sat on the mat.
(@) *The mat sat on the cat.
````

. . .

Pandoc's `(@)` syntax auto-numbers examples continuously across the document:
````markdown
(@ex-good) The manager hired the assistant.
(@ex-bad) *The assistant was hired the manager.

As shown in (@ex-good), the active form is grammatical.
````

## Numbered examples — cross-referencing
````markdown
(@cond-a) Der Dirigent hat den Komponisten *gekannt*, 
          der die Sinfonie geschrieben hat.
(@cond-b) Der Dirigent hat den Komponisten *gekannt*, 
          der gestorben ist.

The stimuli (@cond-a)--(@cond-b) varied in lifetime status.
````

. . .

::: callout-tip
For more control (e.g. (1a), (1b) sub-examples, glosses), use the `linguex` or `gb4e` LaTeX packages in PDF output, or a custom Lua filter for HTML.
:::

---

# Inline reporting {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
*Dynamic values in prose*
:::

## Inline R code

Embed R values directly in prose:
````markdown
The sample comprised `r n_distinct(df$px)` participants
(*M*~age~ = `r round(mean(df$age, na.rm = TRUE), 1)` years).
````

. . .

::: callout-tip
Use `*M*` for italicised statistics in APA style — Quarto renders this correctly in both HTML and PDF.
:::

## Reporting model results inline
````r
# R/utils.R — define once, use everywhere
report_t <- function(model, term) {
  b   <- broom::tidy(model, conf.int = TRUE)
  row <- b[b$term == term, ]
  glue::glue(
    "b = {round(row$estimate, 2)}, ",
    "95% CI [{round(row$conf.low, 2)}, {round(row$conf.high, 2)}], ",
    "t({round(broom::glance(model)$df.residual)}) = {round(row$statistic, 2)}, ",
    "p {ifelse(row$p.value < .001, '< .001', paste0('= ', round(row$p.value, 3)))}"
  )
}
````

. . .
````markdown
The effect of lifetime was significant (`r report_t(m1, "lifetimeDead")`).
````

## APA-style statistics

| Statistic | Markdown | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Mean | `*M* = 310.8` | *M* = 310.8 |
| SD | `*SD* = 155.9` | *SD* = 155.9 |
| t-test | `*t*(48) = 2.31` | *t*(48) = 2.31 |
| p-value | `*p* < .001` | *p* < .001 |
| Cohen's d | `*d* = 0.45` | *d* = 0.45 |

. . .

::: callout-tip
In APA 7, statistical symbols (*M*, *SD*, *t*, *p*, *F*) are italicised. Use `*...*` in Quarto markdown — it works in both HTML and PDF output.
:::

## {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
### ✏️ Your turn

Open `07_writing_exercises.qmd`

1. Add a cross-reference to a figure and a table
2. Write two numbered example sentences and cross-reference them in prose
3. Report a model result inline using `report_t()`
4. Format three statistics in APA style (*M*, *SD*, *p*)

*~15 minutes*
:::

## {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
### 💬 Discuss

*What part of your current writing workflow is most painful?*

. . .

*How could inline reporting or cross-references help?*
:::

## Summary

::: incremental
- Markdown handles most formatting needs
- `csl: apa.csl` gives automatic APA citations
- Cross-references use `@label` syntax with type prefixes
- `(@)` for numbered linguistic examples
- Inline R keeps statistics in sync with your analysis
:::
